narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your low family income bracket, and we appreciate the courage it takes to get this far. The admissions model we use predicted an 80% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was unfortunately rejected—it's designed to flag higher-risk profiles to ensure students are set up for long-term success. This prediction was driven primarily by a few key aspects of your academic record that stood out as the strongest influences. Your LSAT score was the top factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure, as it fell well below the typical applicant's level. Next, your Year 3 school decile ranking, in the bottom tier compared to the average around the middle, reinforced that concern significantly. Your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level versus the usual mid-range, added further weight in the same direction. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA, though slightly below average, helped nudge the prediction a bit toward passing, as did your low family income, which the model saw as a mitigating factor that sometimes correlates with resilience. Being a full-time student aligned with the vast majority of applicants and had only a minor positive influence. Compared to typical students—who often have LSAT scores around 37, mid-level deciles in early and later years, and higher family incomes—your profile in those key academic areas appeared riskier, tipping the scales despite your strengths elsewhere. This isn't a judgment on your potential or worth; it's just the model's data-driven assessment based on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, and gathering letters highlighting your unique perseverance as a Black woman from a low-income background—these steps could dramatically shift future predictions and open doors at other programs. We're rooting for you to rewrite this story.
